contentBlocks.title: When do you need to convert degrees to radians?
contentBlocks.html:
Degrees (°) and radians (rad) are two ways to measure angles. A full circle = 360° = 2π rad. The conversion: radians = degrees × π/180.

Radians are the natural unit for mathematics and physics. JavaScript Math functions (sin, cos, tan) require angles in radians. CSS transforms use degrees.

Common angles: 90° = π/2 rad, 180° = π rad, 45° = π/4 rad, 60° = π/3 rad.

All calculations run locally in your browser — nothing is sent to any server.

contentBlocks.title: How does degrees to radians conversion work?
contentBlocks.html:
The formula is: radians = degrees × π / 180. The reverse: degrees = radians × 180 / π.

This comes from the definition: a full circle = 360° = 2π radians. Therefore, 1° = π/180 ≈ 0.01745 rad.

1 radian ≈ 57.296°. It is the angle where the arc length equals the radius.

contentBlocks.title: Practical tips
contentBlocks.html:
• 0° = 0 rad. 30° = π/6. 45° = π/4. 60° = π/3. 90° = π/2. 180° = π. 360° = 2π.
  • JavaScript: Math.sin(Math.PI / 2) = 1 (sin 90°).
  • CSS uses degrees: transform: rotate(45deg). Canvas uses radians: ctx.rotate(0.785).
  • π ≈ 3.14159265359.

items.question: Why do programming languages use radians?
items.answer: Radians are the natural mathematical unit. The derivatives of sin/cos are simplest in radians. All math libraries standardize on radians.

items.question: How do I use this in JavaScript?
items.answer: Math.sin(degrees * Math.PI / 180). Or create a helper: const toRad = deg => deg * Math.PI / 180.

items.question: Are calculations done locally?
items.answer: Yes. All calculations run in your browser. No data is sent to any server.
